content.problemStatement:
- The **LOOK** disk scheduling algorithm works like **SCAN**, but **instead of going all the way to the end of the disk, it “looks” ahead and stops at the last request** in the current direction before reversing.
- This avoids unnecessary travel to the physical end of the disk when no request is waiting there.

**What This Visualizer Does**
This tool helps you understand how the LOOK algorithm processes disk I/O requests by simulating its step-by-step head movement.

---

**Your Input:**
- Starting disk head position
- A list of track requests (e.g., `98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67`)
- The direction of head movement (Left or Right)

**Visualizer Output:**
- Shows how the disk head moves in the **LOOK** pattern (one direction until the last request in that direction, then reverses).
- Displays the exact order in which requests are served.
- Shows the total head movement for the sequence.
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
